items.text: Every investment is quietly judged against the best thing you could have done instead. A stock likely to return 9% isn't 'making you 9%' if a similar-risk index fund would have made 12% — you're effectively 3% behind. Munger judges every idea against his best available one. Cash, an index fund, and the stocks you already own are all real alternatives competing for the same rupee.

A 'hurdle rate' is a simple rule: 'I won't tie up money for less than X% expected return.' In India, ~12–15% is sensible — comfortably above the ~7% a government bond pays, or you're taking single-stock risk for nothing. The power of a hurdle is in what it makes you skip: the merely-okay ideas that would quietly crowd out better ones later.

The most expensive mistake is invisible: money tied up in a 'fine' idea isn't there when a genuinely great one appears at panic prices. That's why Buffett happily sits on cash that 'earns nothing' — it's really an option to pounce on future bargains. Opportunity cost isn't just comparing today's choices; it's the price of being fully invested when tomorrow's better pitch arrives.
Stock A offers ~9% expected return. An index fund at similar risk offers ~12%. Buying A means:
options: Effectively losing ~3%/yr versus your real alternative
items.explanation: Returns are judged against alternatives foregone. An idea must beat your best available option, not just beat zero.

items.text: Main purpose of a personal hurdle rate (say 14%)?
options: It forces you to skip mediocre ideas so capital is free for exceptional ones
items.explanation: A hurdle is a rejection filter. Its value is in the ideas it screens OUT — protecting capital for asymmetric opportunities.

items.text: Why does Buffett tolerate large cash balances 'earning nothing'?
options: Cash is an option on future bargains — its value appears at panic prices
items.explanation: Cash's return isn't its yield; it's the ability to act when others can't. The 2008-style fat pitch can only be hit with dry powder.

items.text: Open Titan on screener.in and note its P/E. Flip it into an 'earnings yield': 100 ÷ P/E. So a P/E of 50 = a 2% earnings yield. Compare that to the ~7% a bank fixed deposit pays today. If you're accepting 2% now, you're betting profits grow fast enough to justify it. Write one line: what growth would make that trade worth it versus just earning 7% risk-free? (~5 min)
